result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to. I remember the sound of it on the corrugated roof of my grandfather's shed, like a thousand tiny fingers trying to tap their way inside. Now the sky is just this bleached, indifferent thing that hangs over everything like a cheap sheet.

I keep thinking about the bees.

Last summer I found one on the windowsill, legs curled in that particular way that meant it had given up. Not dead yet, but close. I watched it for twenty minutes, its antennae twitching like it was still trying to decode the world. Then it stopped. I buried it under the lavender because it seemed like the right thing to do, though I'm not sure bees have any use for ceremony.

My mother used to say that grief comes in waves but that's a lie. Grief is atmospheric. It's the air itself. You breathe it in until your blood changes composition and suddenly you're this different creature that startles at its own reflection.

I met a man once who told me he could taste colors. Not metaphorically. He said Tuesday was copper and November tasted like rust. I asked him what sadness tasted like and he looked at me for a long time before saying "it doesn't taste like anything. That's how you know it's real."

The parking lot at the old Kmart has become this strange ecosystem. Things grow through the cracks in the asphalt now. Not just weeds. Actual trees. Small ones, sure, but they're reaching. I go there sometimes at dusk when the light is the color of weak tea. Sit on the hood of my car and watch the sky bleed out. The security guard knows me by now. He just nods and keeps walking his route like we're both part of some larger performance that neither of us quite understands.

I have this theory that we're all carrying around these invisible suitcases filled with the versions of ourselves we didn't become. Mine is getting heavy. There's the me who became a marine biologist and the one who learned to play the cello and the one who didn't flinch when his father asked him to be a man. They're all in there, rattling around like loose change.

Sometimes at 3:17am I wake up with the absolute certainty that I'm forgetting something crucial. Not my keys or a deadline. Something bigger. Like how to be a person in the world. The knowledge feels so close I can almost taste it (copper, apparently, according to the synesthete).

My grandmother used to keep a jar of buttons on her dresser. Different sizes, colors, some from coats that no longer existed. She'd let me play with them when I was small, running my fingers through their cool smoothness. I asked her once why she kept them and she said "because everything used to be attached to something and remembering that is important."

I think about that jar a lot lately.

The moon looks different now. Not astronomically. Just to me. It used to feel like a promise. Now it feels like a question I don't have the language to answer. I find myself talking to it sometimes. Not out loud. That would be crazy. Just these silent conversations where I tell it about the bees and the parking lot trees and the man who tasted colors. It never answers but that's fine. Most things don't.

I wonder what the world would look like if we could see all the invisible threads connecting everything. The way my grandmother's buttons used to be part of something larger. The way that dead bee was once part of a hive that was part of an ecosystem that was part of... what? The rain that doesn't fall anymore? The grief that changed my blood?

There's a spider living in the corner of my bathroom. I named her Margaret. She rebuilds her web every single night even though I keep destroying it with the shower spray. I started leaving it alone. Some things deserve their persistence.

I keep a piece of sea glass in my pocket that I found when I was nine. It's the color of nothing that exists in nature anymore, this particular blue-green that feels like memory. I rub my thumb over it when the atmospheric grief gets too thick. It doesn't help, exactly. But it reminds me that some things can be worn smooth by time without losing their essential self.

That's what I want. To be worn smooth. Not erased. Just... simplified. Until all that's left is the essential shape of whatever I actually am under all these suitcases.

The coffee's gone cold again. I do that. Start things and then drift away inside my own head until everything around me turns into evidence. The plants are dying. The laundry is clean but in piles that have achieved geological significance. My friends have stopped calling because what do you say to someone who's clearly conducting an experiment in becoming atmosphere?

But here's the thing I've been avoiding writing:

I think I'm happy.

Not in the loud way. Not even in a way that would be recognizable to most people. It's this quiet, terrible, beautiful thing that feels like standing in the Kmart parking lot watching trees push through concrete. Like Margaret rebuilding her web for the hundredth time. Like carrying around all those unchosen versions of myself and finally understanding they're not luggage, they're geology. They're what made the shape I'm currently wearing.

The rain will probably come back someday. Or it won't. The bees might return or they might not. The sky might stay bleached forever and the moon might keep asking its unanswerable questions.

I'll still be here, mostly. Talking to spiders. Rubbing sea glass. Learning how to be the particular kind of atmospheric that doesn't drown everything it touches.

The jar is full now. All those buttons. All those things that used to be attached to something else.

I think I'm starting to understand what my grandmother meant.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
